title: Bobs Bagels

jokes.text: Do you like this sweater? Jared Leto gave it to me and I've always been on the fence about it.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks a simple fashion question about a sweater
jokes.punchline: The sweater's provenance is a celebrity gift from Jared Leto, delivered as a completely normal qualifier
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis character work — name-dropping Jared Leto as a casual aside is exactly her register. The joke is in the delivery: this is utterly mundane to her. Economy is strong; not a wasted word.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm wearing Valentino.

jokes.text: I like it 'cause Jared Leto gave it to you, and he was my first kiss, but I don't know if I like, like it, like it.
jokes.setup: David is asked for a simple fashion opinion
jokes.punchline: He reveals Jared Leto was HIS first kiss too — an unexpected intimacy — while still being uselessly non-committal on the actual question
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. The Jared Leto first kiss revelation is surprising, funny, and deepens both characters simultaneously. David's inability to give a straight answer even AFTER an extremely personal disclosure is perfectly him. The 'like it, like it' hesitation is also perfectly Gen-Z hedged speech that fits David's register. Multi-layered and highly rewatchable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what do you think? Does this work?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, yeah, it's cute. I mean, I've seen cuter, but it's definitely up there. Like, remember when I was dating that guy from Maroon 5? He had this shirt that was just... *chef's kiss*. Anyway, yours is fine.

jokes.text: I knew this day would come, John! Isolated, impoverished, cut off from all medical services! I only prayed it wouldn't be Alexis.
jokes.setup: Alexis has a cold
jokes.punchline: Moira treats a minor cold as the apocalyptic medical catastrophe she has always feared, complete with dramatic prayer
jokes.explanation: Moira's grandiose catastrophizing is quintessential Moira. The specificity of 'I only prayed it wouldn't be Alexis' implies she was fine with it being David, which lands beautifully. Slightly bloated but the character voice is impeccable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're dying. You're absolutely dying. I can see it in your eyes — that thousand-yard stare of the nearly departed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, I have a cold.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A cold? DAVID. A cold is what people say when they're trying to be brave. You have the plague. We need to start making arrangements. I'm thinking a small, tasteful funeral — nothing too ostentatious, though God knows you'd want me to look stunning in black.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm fine, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Don't be stoic with me. It's unbecoming. Besides, someone needs to tell Alexis you won't be able to help her with her various crises anymore. I assume she'll spiral. We should probably put her on a waiting list for a new sibling now.

jokes.text: It's just a cold, Moira. / Oh, it's always just a cold, John, until it's full blown... Typhoid!
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to calm Moira down about Alexis's cold
jokes.punchline: Moira escalates directly to typhoid as the logical next step
jokes.explanation: The dramatic pause before 'Typhoid!' is a well-structured comedic beat. The word choice of typhoid (not flu, not pneumonia, but a 19th century disease) is perfectly calibrated to Moira's theatrical sense of doom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A cold? Johnny, a cold is what people in Manitoba get. I have a tropical disease. I'm fairly certain I've contracted dengue fever.

jokes.text: Do you remember Valentina? Our chambermaid in little Martinique? We all thought she had just a cold, until it was too late! I'll never shake the mental image of her frothing and flailing in the water taxi!
jokes.setup: Moira is defending her typhoid fear
jokes.punchline: She has a specific, vivid, absurdly theatrical memory of a chambermaid dying dramatically on a water taxi
jokes.explanation: Highly specific dark comedy — 'frothing and flailing in the water taxi' is a genuinely vivid image. The detail of 'little Martinique' and a named chambermaid (Valentina) adds absurd specificity. The comedy escalates from cold → typhoid → dead chambermaid on water taxi in four lines. Strong Moira material.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A cold is not just a cold, darling. It's a gateway to the grave. I once knew a lady's maid who sneezed on a Tuesday and by Friday she was being fitted for a casket. The sneeze led to a sniffle, the sniffle to a cough, the cough to a fever, and the fever to a full celestial departure. She went from folding linens to being folded into them.

jokes.text: I can still hear you!
jokes.setup: Moira dramatically describes Alexis's impending doom while presumably standing just outside her door
jokes.punchline: Alexis's flat interjection reveals the entire dramatic performance was happening within earshot
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical — three words puncture Moira's entire dramatic monologue. The structure is ideal: long build, tiny deflation. Gets a big laugh from the contrast alone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've brought you some juice, darling. How are you feeling?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm fine. It's just a cold.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I should check on Alexis. She mentioned something about needing new hangers.

jokes.text: We love you, Alexis! Don't we?! / We love you, Alexis.
jokes.setup: After being caught talking about Alexis in earshot, Moira pivots to declarations of love
jokes.punchline: She has to prompt Johnny, who then delivers it flatly on command — the love declaration is clearly performative damage control
jokes.explanation: The 'Don't we?!' prompt and Johnny's dutiful echo is a neat bit of marital comedy. The insincerity is the joke. Works well but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a bold choice.

jokes.text: John, don't touch her. / This is as far as I go, Alexis. One of us has to stay safe for David.
jokes.setup: Johnny goes to bring Alexis juice, Moira follows
jokes.punchline: Moira's self-sacrifice is entirely self-interested: she stops at the doorway citing survival duty to David, revealing her real motivation is self-preservation
jokes.explanation: The logic of 'one of us must survive for David' is perfectly Moira — she frames cowardice as maternal sacrifice. The character integration is excellent; this line could only come from Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Is this place actually upscale?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's upscale in the sense that I tell myself it's upscale, and I've gotten very good at believing myself.

jokes.text: I'm already in the room. / See, we're still alive, Moira, I think we're gonna be okay. / Of course you are, that's the spirit. I'm just going to close this door until you're done.
jokes.setup: Johnny is already in Alexis's room, proving Moira's plague fears are overblown
jokes.punchline: Moira cheerfully agrees they'll survive and then immediately closes the door to quarantine them away from her anyway
jokes.explanation: The punchline — closing the door to quarantine them — is a sharp logical extension of Moira's self-serving fear. 'Until you're done' is deliciously vague about whether she means done being sick or done being alive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need emotional support and a ride to the airport.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Can I just do the ride?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, Stevie, I need both.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What if I give you like, half emotional support?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how emotional support works.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, it's the best I can do right now.

jokes.text: Do you like this sweater? / Honestly... While I admire your courage, uh, when it comes to fashion, I'm just not the best person to ask.
jokes.setup: David asks Stevie for a fashion opinion (mirroring the opening scene)
jokes.punchline: Stevie diplomatically sidesteps by admiring his 'courage' rather than actually opining — implying the sweater is baffling
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback to the opening and efficiently establishes the David/Stevie dynamic. 'I admire your courage' is a great diplomatic non-answer. The rewatch bonus comes from seeing the sweater question is David's recurring insecurity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to go change my sweater.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's not going to help.

jokes.text: Mm, you seem stressy. / I'm not. I'm not stressy.
jokes.setup: Stevie observes David's anxiety
jokes.punchline: David's double denial of being 'stressy' — using the childish word — confirms he is absolutely stressy
jokes.explanation: 'Stressy' is a funny invented word that suits both Stevie's offhand observation style and David's over-the-top anxiety. Functional character beat more than a crafted joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, if you were a fly on the wall, you'd be there for about three days before you died.

jokes.text: It's a job interview? For a 'brand manager' position at an upscale boutique. In Elmdale?!
jokes.setup: David reveals he has a job interview
jokes.punchline: Stevie's incredulous 'In Elmdale?!' skewers David's grandiose self-framing of a small-town retail job
jokes.explanation: Sharp beat — the comedic weight is all in the 'In Elmdale?!' The gap between David's self-image and reality is the engine of this show, and this moment efficiently captures it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There are no good bagels in this town.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's because bagels are a New York thing, darling.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what? We could make them here. How hard could it be?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you've never made a bagel in your life.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm serious. There's a market for this. Quality bagels.

jokes.text: Okay, there are certain lies I tell myself, and if you're any kind of a friend, you will let me cling to those lies. And drive me to the interview.
jokes.setup: Stevie has just punctured David's self-delusion about the job
jokes.punchline: David explicitly requests that Stevie participate in his delusion — and also drive him there
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable line — 'there are certain lies I tell myself' is perfectly David: self-aware enough to know he's deluding himself, shameless enough to ask others to enable it. The add-on request for a ride lands as a nice punctuation.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, these muffins are absolutely spectacular! The way the blueberries just burst in your mouth—it's like a symphony of flavors! I've never experienced anything quite like this in my entire life!

jokes.text: So is this like, an either/or type thing? Can I help you cling to lies, and not drive you to Elmdale?
jokes.setup: David has asked Stevie to both validate his delusions AND drive him to Elmdale
jokes.punchline: Stevie tries to negotiate: could she do just the easy part (emotional support) without the inconvenient part (the drive)?
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry negotiation is perfectly in character. The joke is that she's genuinely workshopping whether she can separate the two requests. Good comedic logic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, is your sister alright? She looks positively ghastly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She's just sick, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Sick? I thought she'd simply forgotten her makeup. With Alexis, it's always so difficult to tell.

jokes.text: I'm gonna change my sweater, and meet you at your car, so. / It's like a poncho.
jokes.setup: David declares he'll change his sweater
jokes.punchline: Stevie delivers a parting shot — the sweater is a poncho — with perfect timing as David is leaving
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly timed as a closing shot. 'It's like a poncho' is more devastating than any detailed fashion critique. Economy score is very high — maximum funny per word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not a nurse. I played a psychiatric nurse in Harvey.

jokes.text: So the guy says to me, 'well, I wouldn't try that with a fractured larynx!' I wouldn't try that, period!
jokes.setup: Bob is telling a joke to Roland and Johnny in the office
jokes.punchline: The punchline of Bob's joke-within-a-joke, which lands as amusing but mediocre — and that mediocrity IS the joke about Bob
jokes.explanation: The joke-within-the-joke is deliberately mediocre, establishing Bob as the kind of guy who tells dad jokes in other people's offices. The meta-comedy here is that Bob's joke is not especially funny — and that's the point. Scores accordingly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I would love to stay and comfort you, but I have a choir solo at the community center tonight, and the show must go on.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm dying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, try not to do it before 9 PM.

jokes.text: Morning, boys. / Hi, Johnny. / Ahem! / Oh man! Oh, boy.
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives to find Roland and Bob lounging in his office/space
jokes.punchline: Roland and Bob's sheepish 'Oh man! Oh, boy' — like kids caught by a parent — signals they know they shouldn't be there but couldn't help themselves
jokes.explanation: Inferred visual beat — Johnny walks in on them treating his space as their clubhouse, and their reaction is exactly like caught schoolboys. Works through character and situation but is not a polished joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was traumatic. Roland was here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Shopping with Roland?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. He kept picking things up and asking me if I thought they were 'neat.' I almost had a breakdown in the seasonal section.

jokes.text: Do you guys wanna see a card trick? / Oh, I do. / Okay, first thing I need is a deck of cards.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to start his workday; Roland offers a card trick
jokes.punchline: Roland doesn't have a deck of cards — he's proposing a trick he cannot perform, with full enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland moment — he offers a card trick with nothing to back it up, and Bob is enthusiastically on board before the obvious gap is revealed. The anticlimax is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I accompanied Roland to a big box store. It was like Dante's Inferno, except instead of circles of Hell, there were aisles of industrial-sized mayonnaise. And Roland kept stopping to talk to people he knew, which meant I had to stand there holding a gallon of cooking oil while he discussed someone's colonoscopy results.

jokes.text: Your office? Uh, well that's kind of interesting, Johnny, because I don't remember seeing your application for a new business license.
jokes.setup: Johnny asserts ownership of his office space to kick Roland and Bob out
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately weaponizes bureaucratic procedure — Johnny hasn't registered — to undercut his authority
jokes.explanation: Roland using mayoral authority to needle Johnny is a solid recurring dynamic. Works as character comedy even if the structure isn't particularly tight.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Your family is... you're...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Jewish?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yes! Thank you.

jokes.text: To be a fly on the wall for this conversation! / Bob, you're in the room.
jokes.setup: Bob expresses a wish to observe this conversation
jokes.punchline: Roland points out that Bob IS in the room — he IS the fly on the wall — revealing Bob's complete lack of self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Very sharp and economical. Bob's obliviousness is perfectly captured — he's somehow narrating a scene he's participating in. The structure is elegant: absurd setup, devastatingly simple corrective punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I just want to say, I have nothing against people of your... background.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: My background?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you know, you're... you're very...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm very what, Bob?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Ethnic. In a good way! The best way. I love ethnic people. Some of my best friends are... ethnic.

jokes.text: You know, Johnny, it's kind of standard procedure for new businesses to register. But then again, I guess you don't really have a lot to register, do you?
jokes.setup: Roland is ostensibly being helpful about business registration
jokes.punchline: The offer of procedure advice becomes a dig at Johnny's lack of any actual business
jokes.explanation: Competent Roland needle but not particularly original. Works within the running dynamic of Roland condescending to Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you can say it, but... I don't know. There's just something about the way you say it that makes me feel weird.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What do you mean? It's just a word.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But when you say 'Jewish,' it just... it sounds so... Jewish.

jokes.text: I did start Rose Video with $2,000 and a dream, so it's all about planning. / I see it every day. Lots of quiet planning. Sure, he treats himself to a muffin or two, but uh, I get that that's all part of the planning, right, Johnny? / Could be.
jokes.setup: Johnny defends his inactivity as 'planning'
jokes.punchline: Bob enthusiastically co-signs the delusion, specifically noting the muffins as 'part of the planning' — and Johnny lamely concedes it 'could be'
jokes.explanation: This is subtly painful comedy — Bob means it kindly but perfectly exposes that Johnny has been doing nothing but eating muffins. 'Could be' is Johnny's defeated acceptance that this is what his life has become. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: Store Owner
dialogue.line: So I see here you called my store a 'sad, fluorescent-lit tomb of broken dreams' on social media.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: I was having a bad day!

dialogue.speaker: Store Owner
dialogue.line: You were standing in my store when you posted it.

jokes.text: I'd kill for a bagel, but I haven't seen one bagel since I got to this town. Now, back at Rose Video, I used to get my assistant to bring me a bagel every morning. So I might think a bagel shop is something this town could use.
jokes.setup: Johnny is musing about what business he could start
jokes.punchline: His entire 'business idea' is transparently derived from missing his old life's luxury — having a personal assistant fetch his bagel — now repackaged as visionary entrepreneurship
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the self-revelation: Johnny thinks he's being a visionary entrepreneur, but we can see he just wants his old life back. The assistant detail makes it specific and class-aware.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I've accepted that 'skanky' is now part of our vernacular.

jokes.text: Ooh, I just get chills thinking that we're sitting right in the middle of the 'Johnny Rose Dream Factory!'
jokes.setup: Johnny has talked about his past success to set up his non-existent bagel idea
jokes.punchline: Bob names the empty space 'the Johnny Rose Dream Factory' with complete sincerity, making the gap between grandeur and reality funny
jokes.explanation: Bob's unironic sincerity makes this funny — he genuinely believes he is witnessing genius in a municipal office. 'Dream Factory' is an absurdly grandiose name for what is functionally a table in a small-town building. Quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think 'skanky' is a bit of an overstatement. I'd say it's more... functionally challenged. The store has character. Character that could be vastly improved with literally everything replaced.

jokes.text: Ooooh! Stupid... baby.
jokes.setup: Roland reacts to Bob's 'Dream Factory' enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: Roland's barely-suppressed eye-roll — 'Stupid... baby' — suggests he finds Bob's fawning over Johnny embarrassing but can't quite say it properly
jokes.explanation: Inferred reaction beat — Roland's muttered non-insult 'stupid baby' is a funny character tic. The joke is in the inadequacy of his comeback. Moderate laugh, character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm trying to be helpful here, but I have to say, that color really doesn't flatter you. It makes you look a bit washed out. And the fit is rather unflattering. But you know, if you're happy with it, that's what matters.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Also, have you considered doing something different with your hair? Because the way you're wearing it now really emphasizes your features in a way that's not entirely advantageous. Not that there's anything wrong with your features, of course. They're just very... prominent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I don't mean to criticize, but your taste in general seems to lean toward choices that really don't showcase your best qualities. It's almost like you're actively working against yourself. But I'm sure you're doing the best you can.

jokes.text: I'll tell you what, Johnny, instead of the license fee, I'll just take a bite of your muffin. Mmm! Good! Mmmm! Mm! Mm!
jokes.setup: Roland is positioned as the bureaucratic gatekeeper who could demand a license fee
jokes.punchline: Instead he extracts payment in the form of Johnny's muffin — and then commits to eating it with exaggerated relish, making it a power move disguised as a favor
jokes.explanation: Roland taking the muffin as currency is a great character beat — it reduces 'business licensing' to a muffin. The extended 'mmm's are presumably performed with physical comedy. Roland-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ivan
dialogue.line: Hello, I am Ivan. I am very happy to be here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, welcome. We're glad to have you.

dialogue.speaker: Ivan
dialogue.line: I must tell you, your business, it is very... how do you say... shit?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Ivan
dialogue.line: Your business, it is shit. Very shit.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh, no, no. I think there's been a misunderstanding.

jokes.text: I have to go to this Jazzagals rehearsal, Alexis, so I'll need your word you'll remain stable. We're a long way from any hospitals. You can't be playing fast and loose with those symptoms.
jokes.setup: Moira checks on Alexis before leaving
jokes.punchline: Moira's farewell is not 'feel better' but a medical directive and a reminder of how far they are from help — she's dramatizing a cold as a survival situation
jokes.explanation: 'Playing fast and loose with those symptoms' is a delightful Moira phrase — medically dramatic and utterly her. Character integration is the main strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was just making conversation about bagels. It was theoretical.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I've been thinking about it, and I have a solution. We cut the bagels in half.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: That way, one bagel becomes two bagels.

jokes.text: Alexis? — Alexis?! — I'm sleeping!
jokes.setup: Moira has been checking on Alexis while also worried about leaving for choir rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira calls Alexis's name twice with escalating panic; Alexis's irritated 'I'm sleeping!' deflates the dramatic moment instantly
jokes.explanation: Functional comedic beat. Moira's escalating panic deflated by Alexis simply being asleep. Economy is good but the joke is light.

jokes.text: Oh, honey! Honey, I need to ask you a serious question. Has it gotten worse, or are you just not wearing any makeup?
jokes.setup: Moira sees Alexis looking ill and approaches with dramatic concern
jokes.punchline: Her 'serious question' is whether Alexis looks bad due to illness or simply no makeup — Moira genuinely cannot distinguish between the two
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The structure is elegant: 'serious question' sets up genuine concern, then the punchline reveals that Moira's concern is entirely aesthetic. The character integration is near-perfect — only Moira would ask this, and it reveals exactly how she processes the world. Highly quotable and lands a big laugh. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The French have a saying: 'If your clothes fit, you're not French enough.'

jokes.text: I think you have to do it without the tissue. / You're right, okay, put your hand up on the forehead. / Mom! / Oh God!
jokes.setup: Moira tries to feel Alexis's forehead through a tissue to avoid contact
jokes.punchline: The tissue method fails; touching bare forehead is treated as a heroic sacrifice; Moira's 'Oh God!' suggests it's genuinely traumatic for her
jokes.explanation: The visual of Moira trying to take a temperature through a tissue is a good physical comedy beat. The escalation from tissue attempt to bare-hand horror is well-structured. Character integration strong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, the French have a saying: 'La vie est courte, profitez-en.' Life is short, enjoy it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not French, David. That's just you trying to sound interesting at a dinner party.

jokes.text: It's warm! / How warm? / I don't know Alexis, I'm not a nurse! Though I played one once in a lovely little production of 'Harvey,' but she worked in a mental institute.
jokes.setup: Moira says Alexis is warm; Alexis asks how warm
jokes.punchline: Moira deflects with 'I'm not a nurse!' then immediately undercuts it with the revelation she DID play a nurse — but in a mental institute, which undermines the medical authority anyway
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira joke. The structure is a double pivot: 'I'm not a nurse' (reasonable), undercut by 'though I played one' (absurd), undercut again by 'in a mental institute' (making the acting credential useless anyway). Three beats, perfectly executed. The 'lovely little production of Harvey' is a bonus detail that deepens the absurdity. Near-perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What do you think of this dress?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think it's a bit... revealing, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's fashion, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a cry for help.

jokes.text: I blame those late nights at the barn. Cold air whistling in and out, and you and Mutt not wearing near enough clothing.
jokes.setup: Moira speculates on why Alexis got sick
jokes.punchline: She pivots from medical concern to passive-aggressive commentary on Alexis's relationship/lifestyle choices — the real subtext is disapproval of Mutt
jokes.explanation: A gentle Moira passive-aggressive dig. Works as character texture but the punchline isn't sharp enough to be a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: How does this look?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, it's lovely, though it does remind me of what I wore to my arraignment in '09.

jokes.text: Everybody gets sick sometimes. / Speak for yourself!
jokes.setup: Alexis offers a universal truth to ground Moira
jokes.punchline: Moira rejects the universal — she apparently doesn't get sick, positioning herself as constitutionally superior
jokes.explanation: 'Speak for yourself!' in three words establishes Moira's belief in her own magnificence. Maximum economy — the joke is entirely in the refusal to include herself in the category of 'everybody.' Very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: This outfit is absolutely perfect! David really knows what he's doing.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: I'm wearing it to my husband's criminal trial next week.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Oh, how wonderful! What a great occasion to debut a new look.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Alexis, I'm sorry I must leave you, but... mummy's gotta fight for her solo.
jokes.setup: Moira makes her farewell after a quasi-tender moment
jokes.punchline: The dramatically maternal 'mummy' framing crashes into the trivial self-interested reason she's leaving: a choir solo
jokes.explanation: The third-person 'mummy's gotta' framing combined with 'fight for her solo' perfectly encapsulates Moira's self-prioritization dressed up as theatrical sacrifice. Good economy and strong character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, I see. Well, don't mind me. I'll just be here, doing everything myself, as I always do. Completely fine. Absolutely fine.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, Mutt is just helping me with—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You two go ahead. I wouldn't want to be in the way. I'm used to being invisible in my own family anyway.

jokes.text: No. Nope. No. / What? / Well, this can't be it. / That's it.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie arrive at the boutique for the job interview
jokes.punchline: David's triple refusal to accept the reality of where he is (a small-town store), Stevie's flat confirmation
jokes.explanation: David's escalating denial ('No. Nope. No.') is a good character beat. The economy of the Stevie comeback 'That's it.' is strong. Effective but not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You can't just pick and choose when you want to be a mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a busy woman, David. If I scheduled motherhood like I schedule my facials, we'd all be better off.

jokes.text: There is a solid chance I may have insulted the owner. / Oh well, that doesn't sound like something you'd do. / She has very questionable taste. And I may or may not have told her that to her face.
jokes.setup: David reveals a potential obstacle to the interview
jokes.punchline: Stevie's sarcastic 'that doesn't sound like something you'd do' is confirmed immediately by David admitting he told the owner to her face — the non-apology explanation 'she has very questionable taste' makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: Stevie's sarcasm is perfectly calibrated — she already knows exactly what David is like. David's explanation 'she has very questionable taste' as if that fully justifies the insult is excellent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I wouldn't say never. I bought you medicine once.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That time you had a yeast infection and I was out of Pellegrino.

jokes.text: Also, this is probably the only job in this town that you'll ever be qualified for. / Okay, so do you want me to lead with that, or hold it 'til the end, and keep it as a surprise?
jokes.setup: Stevie gives David a harsh pep talk about why he should go in
jokes.punchline: David reframes Stevie's brutal assessment as interview talking-point strategy — an absurdist take on a genuinely devastating piece of feedback
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The structure is impeccable: Stevie delivers a genuinely cutting observation, and David's response treats it as strategic ammunition rather than an insult. The 'lead with it or hold it as a surprise' framing is absurdist and beautifully in-character — David using ego as armor. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, Alexis, darling. You look absolutely drained. Have you been tested for mono? I only ask because, well, given your... extensive extracurricular activities, it does seem rather likely.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? I'm simply expressing concern for your health. Mutt, you might want to get yourself checked as well.

jokes.text: Because you're uh... you... / Jewish? / I didn't know if I could say it.
jokes.setup: Bob is building to the point that Johnny would know how to make 'real' bagels
jokes.punchline: Bob can't bring himself to say 'Jewish' — Johnny has to say it for him — and Bob's explanation ('I didn't know if I could say it') reveals his anxious awkwardness around the word
jokes.explanation: This is sharp social comedy. Bob's well-meaning but completely awkward avoidance of the word 'Jewish' — forcing Johnny to say it — is both cringe-funny and observationally rich. The beat where Bob pauses ('you're uh... you...') and Johnny fills in the blank is excellently written and performed. 'I didn't know if I could say it' is the payoff. Strong structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I think you might have feelings for him.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, I would rather eat glass.

jokes.text: But boy, do you all love your bagels! I mean I do too, and I'm not even uh...
jokes.setup: Bob has just been helped through saying 'Jewish' by Johnny
jokes.punchline: Bob immediately deploys a broad ethnic generalization ('you all love your bagels') and then trails off unable to say 'Jewish' AGAIN when referring to himself as a non-example
jokes.explanation: The callback to Bob's inability to say 'Jewish' is handled brilliantly — he commits the very ethnic faux pas he was trying to avoid ('you all love your bagels') and then immediately can't say the word AGAIN. The structure of the escalation is excellent. 'You all love your bagels' is delivered with such earnest positivity that it lands as cringe comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't want to use 'Bob'.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, is it because I'm Jewish?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, it's not that at all.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Because I can change it. I can be something else.

jokes.text: Jewish. You can say it, Bob. / I don't know why, it feels like a swear. / What's the one you can't say?
jokes.setup: Johnny again has to supply the word 'Jewish' for Bob
jokes.punchline: Bob confesses it 'feels like a swear' — and then inexplicably asks what THE swear word is that can't be said, suggesting he has some other word in mind
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the running gag peaks here. 'It feels like a swear' is funny, but 'What's the one you can't say?' is the knockout — implying Bob has an even worse word in his mental taxonomy. Johnny's horrified exit from the conversation is implied. Three-beat escalation delivered with perfect economy. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I was going to open a bagel shop.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I had it all planned out. The location, the menu, the hours...

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I even mentally invested $50,000 into it.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: And now I'm out that money.

jokes.text: So... David! This is quite the resume. If I ever need a performance artist I know who to call. / Thank you.
jokes.setup: Wendy reviews David's resume for a retail job
jokes.punchline: The backhanded 'if I ever need a performance artist' makes clear his resume is useless for this context; David's sincere 'Thank you' suggests he takes it as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Clean irony: Wendy's sarcasm goes over David's head (or he chooses to accept it). The 'Thank you' is the punchline. Works efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Getting champagne to celebrate? What's the occasion?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're celebrating the fact that nobody's currently broken up or estranged.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So... we're celebrating a Tuesday?

jokes.text: Skanky! / Excuse me? / You called my store skanky when you were here with the mayor.
jokes.setup: The interview seems to be going normally
jokes.punchline: Wendy blindsides David by dropping 'Skanky!' mid-interview, revealing she remembers exactly what he said about her store
jokes.explanation: The timing of 'Skanky!' dropped right after the polite resume exchange is excellent comedy structure. It's a narrative trap David walked into, and the audience can feel his panic. Good cringe setup.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm adding a raisin to your champagne.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's fancy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not fancy, Stevie. It's a raisin. In champagne.

jokes.text: What I think I'd bring to the position is a worldly approach to merchandising, and sales tactics. / We're not over the skanky thing, are we? / No.
jokes.setup: David tries to pivot to his qualifications
jokes.punchline: He can't help himself — he metacommentates on the still-open wound mid-pitch, and Wendy confirms it's not resolved
jokes.explanation: David's self-sabotaging awareness — he knows it's not done but can't stop himself from checking — is perfectly in character. The 'No.' from Wendy is a clean punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: To David's first job. I remember my first job. I was a child model.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you were a child model for like, five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It was a very formative five minutes.

jokes.text: I can't help but wonder, why you'd wanna work in a store you described as skanky! / I don't know if we need to keep saying the word. / Skanky!
jokes.setup: Wendy presses the skanky wound; David tries to retire the word
jokes.punchline: Wendy immediately repeats 'Skanky!' with even more emphasis — refusing to let David manage the language
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of David trying to suppress the word and Wendy triumphantly re-deploying it is excellent. The joke is in the power dynamic — Wendy owns this situation completely. 'Skanky!' as a punctuating exclamation is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my darling Alexis, you are the light of my life, the apple of my eye, the very reason I wake up in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Unlike your brother David, who is a constant source of disappointment and really brings down the energy of this family.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But you, sweetheart, you make it all worthwhile. Mostly because people often comment on how beautiful you are, and since you're my daughter, it reflects rather well on me.

jokes.text: Which is why I feel like I can help. / You think I need your help?! / Well, I am answering an ad in the paper.
jokes.setup: David pivots from 'skanky' to claiming he can help improve the store
jokes.punchline: Wendy is offended; David's comeback — 'Well, I am answering an ad in the paper' — is a devastating logical point: if she didn't need help, why was she advertising?
jokes.explanation: David's logic is impeccable and the economy is perfect. 'Well, I am answering an ad in the paper' is a genuinely sharp comeback — factually undeniable and mildly insulting to Wendy's self-perception. It's the rare moment where David wins a verbal exchange. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, sweetie, could you adjust the humidity in here? My hair is starting to frizz and I can feel a polyp forming.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, we're having a moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know, and it's lovely, but my lower back is starting to spasm and I think my sciatica is flaring up. Also, is it hot in here or is it just me experiencing early-onset menopausal symptoms?

jokes.text: Is the floor overcrowded? Yeah. Are the mannequins a little too busty? Absolutely. Does it smell like urinal cakes in here? Perhaps.
jokes.setup: David attempts to frame his criticisms as 'constructive' feedback to Wendy
jokes.punchline: The escalation from floor overcrowding → busty mannequins → urinal cake smell — with David's measured hedges ('Absolutely,' 'Perhaps') — becomes increasingly absurd
jokes.explanation: This is excellent escalating character comedy. The trio of criticisms escalates perfectly: mildly aesthetic → weird specific detail (busty mannequins) → genuinely offensive sensory observation (urinal cakes). David's calibrated qualifiers ('Yeah,' 'Absolutely,' 'Perhaps') give it perfect rhythm. 'Does it smell like urinal cakes in here? Perhaps.' is an instant quotable line. Strong craft. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: It seems as though you have trouble filtering negative thoughts. So I don't know if I can trust you to represent the 'Blouse Barn.'
jokes.setup: Wendy processes David's stream of insults framed as feedback
jokes.punchline: She diagnoses him with an inability to filter — which is accurate — and the store name 'Blouse Barn' lands as its own punchline: the place called the Blouse Barn is worried about reputation
jokes.explanation: The name 'Blouse Barn' is itself a joke — it's a comedically unglamorous name that clashes with Wendy's delusions of upscale boutique status. Wendy's diagnosis of David is also accurate and funny. Solid.

jokes.text: Can I help you? / Bob wants us to meet. / Bob wants us to meet?!
jokes.setup: A large, unknown man (Ivan) appears in Johnny's office claiming Bob sent him
jokes.punchline: Johnny's incredulous repetition — 'Bob wants us to meet?!' — conveys exactly how little he trusts Bob's matchmaking at this point
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat. Johnny's disbelief at Bob's escalating interference is well-played. Not a standout joke but serves the scene.

jokes.text: Well, there's no bagel — I mean, you know, Bob, I already told you... / I got some brick. / You've got bricks? What bricks? / For bagel oven.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to shut down the bagel idea
jokes.punchline: Ivan has already sourced bricks for a bagel oven — the plan has moved forward without Johnny's involvement
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the bagel idea running away from Johnny is comedically satisfying. Ivan's matter-of-fact 'For bagel oven' as if this is obvious is a strong deadpan beat.

jokes.text: Bob, you know, this bagel shop idea was just an idea. You know, it's not even an idea, it's just an illustration of a theoretical idea.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to walk back the bagel shop
jokes.punchline: His increasingly desperate qualification — from 'just an idea' to 'illustration of a theoretical idea' — reveals the extent of his scrambling
jokes.explanation: 'An illustration of a theoretical idea' is a great piece of corporate-speak used in increasingly dire circumstances. Johnny's rhetorical escalation while backing away from a nonexistent business plan is excellent.

jokes.text: Could you stand, and excuse us? / All right, oh, okay. / I'll meet you out there in a minute. / Bob, you know, we are... so f... Oh. We are so far from even remotely having a conversation about this... You know, business.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to get Ivan out of the room to confront Bob
jokes.punchline: Johnny almost swears — catches himself — and lands on the most bureaucratically polite version of 'we are screwed' possible
jokes.explanation: Johnny's almost-swear and recovery is a good physical/verbal comedy beat. The polite circumlocution after nearly saying something rude is relatable and character-specific.

jokes.text: You said you started Rose Video with $2,000 bucks and a dream. / Bob, you-you don't have to... / Let's turn this dream... Into green!
jokes.setup: Bob is using Johnny's own inspirational origin story to justify his investment
jokes.punchline: 'Let's turn this dream... Into green!' — Bob's motivational slogan is as earnest as it is clichéd, and the pause before 'Into green' suggests he's very proud of it
jokes.explanation: Bob's slogan lands because of his complete sincerity. 'Let's turn this dream... into green!' is the kind of thing someone says when they think they've coined a phrase. The implied pause before 'Into green!' adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: Bob, I don't want your check. / Oh my God.
jokes.setup: Bob has presented a check; Johnny refuses it
jokes.punchline: Bob's 'Oh my God' — presumably a genuine expression of bewilderment that someone would refuse his investment — is a perfect character beat
jokes.explanation: Short reaction beat. Bob's shock at money being refused is characterful. Not a standout but effective.

jokes.text: In France, they say the looser the fit, the sexier the feel, so... / I've never been to France.
jokes.setup: David is selling a customer a loose-fitting garment using a made-up French saying
jokes.punchline: The customer (Stevie) flatly deflates the entire premise with 'I've never been to France'
jokes.explanation: David's invention of a French fashion aphorism is exactly his register. Stevie's 'I've never been to France' perfectly deflates it — not by challenging its truth, but by making the cultural authority claim irrelevant. Good economy.

jokes.text: Um, yeah, I'm gonna need you to do something for me. / Like drive home without you? / Uh no, Wendy has got me on a bit of a trial run with the customers, and I'm gonna need you to help me out by looking like you're enjoying this conversation a little more than you are, um, I'm gonna need you to buy a couple blouses from me.
jokes.setup: David pulls Stevie aside for help during the trial run
jokes.punchline: His ask escalates from 'look enthusiastic' to 'buy two blouses' — Stevie's 'Like drive home without you?' is a preemptive guess at David's audacity
jokes.explanation: Stevie's preemptive 'drive home without you?' is a great beat — she already knows David is about to ask something unreasonable. The revelation of 'two blouses' is appropriately anticlimactic but escalates to the actual inconvenience.

jokes.text: I'm gonna need you to help me out by looking like you're enjoying this conversation a little more than you are, um, I'm gonna need you to buy a couple blouses from me.
jokes.setup: David ropes Stevie into his trial shift.
jokes.punchline: David's first ask ('look like you're enjoying this') is already a lot, then the second ask ('buy a couple blouses') escalates the favor considerably.
jokes.explanation: Functional comedic escalation. The ask grows in unreasonableness, which is a reliable comedic pattern. Character-specific but not particularly original.

jokes.text: A couple?! / One. A blouse.
jokes.setup: Stevie protests having to buy two blouses
jokes.punchline: David immediately backtracks — 'One. A blouse.' — the haggling punctured instantly
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. 'One. A blouse.' is David immediately caving in the most crisp, itemized way possible. Three words of comedy gold.

jokes.text: What about this? / Well, that's not an option. / That-no. / But I have my sister's communion this weekend.
jokes.setup: Stevie finds a blouse she wants
jokes.punchline: David shoots it down categorically; Stevie attempts the communal emotional appeal (sister's communion), which gets no traction
jokes.explanation: Functional joke. David's 'That-no' is a good stutter of horror. Stevie's sister's communion is a nice absurd detail for the appeal. Not memorable but works.

jokes.text: Okay, don't do that. / Don't do what? / Do you not like it? Don't, Stevie! / Good recommendation, sir! / This is gonna be a smash at my husband's trial hearing. / He's got good taste.
jokes.setup: David catches Stevie about to ask another customer for an opinion on the blouse
jokes.punchline: A random customer enthusiastically endorses the blouse for his wife to wear to his trial hearing — a darkly absurd detail delivered casually
jokes.explanation: 'This is gonna be a smash at my husband's trial hearing' is a great dark comedy non-sequitur. The character exists only to deliver this line, and the detail of the 'trial hearing' — which nobody questions — is perfectly understated. David's straight-faced 'He's got good taste' as if this is normal is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Alexis? The woman at the general store said a girl in Elmdale recently contracted avian flu. So I got you a bit of everything.
jokes.setup: Moira returns to Alexis's room with supplies
jokes.punchline: Her medical intelligence-gathering at the general store has led to an avian flu scare, and she's bought a prepper's spread of remedies 'just in case'
jokes.explanation: Moira's escalation from typhoid fears to avian flu is consistent with her character but not a new joke — it's a continuation of the established dynamic. Works through performance more than writing.

jokes.text: No, I-I'm relieved that Alexis has someone with such natural care-giving tendencies.
jokes.setup: Moira arrives to find someone else caring for Alexis
jokes.punchline: Moira frames her own negligence as relief that others have 'natural care-giving tendencies' — she's complimenting the caregiver while excusing herself
jokes.explanation: Moira's ability to make her own absence sound like virtuous delegation is characteristically her. Not a big laugh moment but strong character texture.

jokes.text: Had I known your spirits were so high, I might not have spent the last 45 minutes of my life running around buying cold medication. / I didn't ask you to go buy cold medication. / When have you ever bought me cold medication?!
jokes.setup: Moira is annoyed that Alexis is well-enough to have company
jokes.punchline: Alexis's rebuke — 'When have you ever bought me cold medication?!' — exposes that this is the first time Moira has done anything vaguely nurturing, making her complaint absurd
jokes.explanation: The punchline is devastating character work — Alexis lands a genuinely revelatory line that exposes the absurdity of Moira's complaint. 'When have you EVER bought me cold medication?!' lands as both funny and emotionally textured. Good structure; the setup earns the comeback.

jokes.text: Exactly, I knew it didn't feel right.
jokes.setup: Alexis has established that Moira has never bought her cold medication before
jokes.punchline: Moira agrees — 'I knew it didn't feel right' — framing her instinctive resistance to buying medicine as a kind of self-awareness, not neglect
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed single-line joke. The callback is perfect: Alexis exposes Moira's neglect, and Moira IMMEDIATELY repurposes that exposure as self-knowledge. 'I knew it didn't feel right' implies Moira's body rejected the act of purchasing cold medicine as instinctively wrong for her. It's absurd, perfectly in-character, and devastatingly delivered. High quotability. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Oh dear, are we sure this isn't just mono again? What with your symptoms, and your lifestyle? / Mrs. Rose, I don't have mono. / Oh no offense dear, could be others.
jokes.setup: Moira assesses Alexis's condition in front of Alexis's companion
jokes.punchline: 'Oh no offense dear, could be others' — Moira is suggesting the companion might have given Alexis mono, which is both a sexual insinuation and a completely socially inappropriate thing to say
jokes.explanation: Moira dropping 'could be others' to the companion — implying they might be the mono vector — is social dynamite delivered with perfect aristocratic insouciance. The 'Oh no offense dear' setup makes the following line more devastating. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Weren't you supposed to be at choir practice? / Rehearsal. Yes. I supposed to be there over an hour ago. / So why aren't you? / Because I kept picturing you diseased and all alone.
jokes.setup: Moira corrects 'choir practice' to 'rehearsal' with full dignity, then admits she should have left an hour ago
jokes.punchline: The reason she stayed is genuinely maternal — 'I kept picturing you diseased and all alone' — which is both touching and hilariously dramatic
jokes.explanation: The correction of 'choir practice' to 'Rehearsal' is a character laugh. But the real payoff is 'diseased and all alone' — Moira's love is real but filtered through her dramatic vocabulary. A warm moment with genuine comedic texture.

jokes.text: That's never stopped you before. / I know! / What the hell is going on, Alexis!
jokes.setup: Alexis points out that Moira's love has never previously stopped her from leaving
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I know!' is a gleeful admission of her own prior negligence — followed immediately by her own bafflement at her new behavior
jokes.explanation: 'I know!' delivered as enthusiastic agreement with the accusation of maternal neglect is one of the episode's warmest comedy beats. Moira is simultaneously admitting her past failures AND baffled by her own growth. The 'What the hell is going on, Alexis!' confirms she finds her own maternal feelings disorienting and hilarious. Strong character work.

jokes.text: I'm feeling something. Something debilitating! You're the ill one, but I'm... I'm feeling this... This... / Maternal instinct, maybe? / No, that's not it. / No! / Maybe. / No!
jokes.setup: Moira tries to articulate her unprecedented maternal feelings
jokes.punchline: Alexis names it (maternal instinct); Moira rejects it twice while a small 'Maybe' breaks through — perfectly capturing her internal war between her self-image and her actual feelings
jokes.explanation: The best comedic moment in the episode. The rhythm of 'No. No! Maybe. No!' is perfect — Moira's three-denial-with-crack is exquisitely structured. The joke works because it's simultaneously hilarious and emotionally true: Moira IS feeling maternal instinct but is constitutionally unable to admit it. The comedy and the character growth are inseparable. This is what Schitt's Creek does better than almost any other show — humor that makes you feel something. Justifies 8.7+ craft and 9.0 impact.

jokes.text: I assumed I would be consulted before we made any major decisions about the bagel business. / What decisions?! / Well, hiring Ivan for one thing, I don't have good feeling about that guy. I think he's ex-KGB.
jokes.setup: Roland is upset about being excluded from the bagel business he knows nothing about
jokes.punchline: Roland's reason for distrusting Ivan is that he suspects him of being ex-KGB — an absurd Cold War-era profiling based on... his name? His demeanor?
jokes.explanation: Roland's ex-KGB suspicion is perfectly him — paranoid, culturally oblivious, delivered with total conviction. It's also the right kind of absurd: plausible as something Roland would believe, and funny because Ivan is just a quarry worker.

jokes.text: Roland, there is no bagel business! Okay, it was just a stupid idea I made up because you were right. / Well, I know that! / Right about what?
jokes.setup: Johnny confesses the bagel idea was invented to fill a void
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately confirms 'I know that!' — but then reveals he has no idea what he was right about — his confidence is entirely reflexive
jokes.explanation: Roland's reflexive 'I know that!' followed immediately by 'Right about what?' is a great bit of character comedy — his confidence is completely hollow. The structure of agree-first-then-reveal-you-don't-know is solid.

jokes.text: Look at this, the brain trust! / So I was thinking, and uh, I'm not married to it, but uh... 'Bob's Bagels.'
jokes.setup: Bob arrives while Johnny is having a real conversation with Roland
jokes.punchline: 'Bob's Bagels' — Bob has named the business after himself, for a business Johnny just admitted is not real, pitching it as if it's a casual suggestion he's 'not married to'
jokes.explanation: Bob's casual self-naming of the business — with the caveat 'I'm not married to it' — is perfectly character-driven comedy. His obliviousness to the conversation happening is itself the joke.

jokes.text: Johnny, I think you have something you'd like to say to Bob. / You don't wanna use the name Bob? / Oh, I get it, go with something a little more old testament?
jokes.setup: Roland pushes Johnny to tell Bob the truth; Bob mishears and thinks the problem is his name being on the business
jokes.punchline: Bob's pivot — assuming they want a more 'old testament' name, i.e. a Jewish-sounding name — hilariously revives the Jewish subtext from the earlier scene
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Jewish conversation is smart writing — Bob's awkwardness around Jewishness resurfaces in a new context where it creates an additional layer of awkward. 'Something a little more old testament' is a wonderfully clumsy construction. Well-integrated callback.

jokes.text: You would've given me money? / No, of course not, but as your partner... / You're not a partner! Not my partner.
jokes.setup: Roland reproaches Johnny for taking Bob's money without consulting him
jokes.punchline: When Johnny points out Roland wouldn't have given him money anyway, Roland confirms this — but insists on the partnership status without the financial component
jokes.explanation: Roland wanting the title of 'partner' with none of the investment is characteristically absurd. Johnny's emphatic 'Not my partner' is the right beat to end on.

jokes.text: You know, next time uh... I should probably see a business plan so I don't invest so much in another uh, hollow scheme. / Yeah, me too. / You didn't invest. / Yes, and whose fault is that?
jokes.setup: Bob delivers a mild reproach about the lack of a business plan
jokes.punchline: Roland somehow converts his exclusion from the non-existent investment as a grievance — 'You didn't invest. / Yes, and whose fault is that?' — implying Johnny wronged him by not letting him lose money
jokes.explanation: Roland's logic is gloriously perverse — he's aggrieved that he was not allowed to invest in a fake business. 'Yes, and whose fault is that?' treats being excluded from a bad investment as an injustice. This is a beautifully absurd escalation. Justifies 8+ — the joke requires precise character and the structure of the exchange is tight.

jokes.text: Mm... / Well, congratulations. / Let me get the champagne. You guys are gonna love it, it's the same one we use to celebrate my mom's divorces.
jokes.setup: David has apparently been hired at the Blouse Barn and is celebrating
jokes.punchline: David's champagne is 'the same one we use to celebrate my mom's divorces' — casual, dark, and deeply revealing about the Rose family's complicated history
jokes.explanation: 'The same one we use to celebrate my mom's divorces' is casually dark and highly quotable. The implication that there are enough divorces to have a dedicated celebration protocol — and a specific champagne — says everything about the Rose family. Excellent character detail with strong comedic economy.

jokes.text: Great... / So how long before I can return this? / Oh, it's exchange or store credit only. / That's funny. / I'm not joking.
jokes.setup: Stevie wants to return the blouse she just bought
jokes.punchline: David enforces the store's return policy on his friend without hesitation; Stevie thinks he's joking; he is not
jokes.explanation: The exchange-only policy landing on Stevie is a neat ironic punchline to the blouse subplot. 'That's funny. / I'm not joking.' is a well-timed beat. David going full retail mode on his own friend is characteristically him.

jokes.text: Well, I guess you're buying drinks tonight. / I can do that, that's fair. / And dinner. / Okay. / And if I feel like dessert... / It's a $12 dollar negligee on a 2-for-1 promotion so...
jokes.setup: Stevie negotiates compensation for being forced to buy a blouse
jokes.punchline: David agrees to drinks, then dinner, then starts to push back on dessert — revealed when he mentions the negligee was $12 on a 2-for-1 promotion, making all of Stevie's demands wildly disproportionate
jokes.explanation: The reveal of the $12 price tag on a 2-for-1 promo retroactively makes Stevie's growing list of demands absurd — she's treating a six-dollar blouse purchase like a major sacrifice. Good escalation.

jokes.text: Okay, I'll just drop a little raisin in here to release the bubbles. / Blechhh! That's not necessary! / Okay!
jokes.setup: David opens champagne for the celebration
jokes.punchline: Wendy's violent 'Blechhh! That's not necessary!' in response to the raisin trick is an exaggerated reaction to a minor champagne custom
jokes.explanation: Mild physical comedy beat. Wendy's squeamishness about the raisin is charming but not a standout joke. Works as a character texture moment.

jokes.text: Congratulations! / A whole raisin.
jokes.setup: They're celebrating with champagne
jokes.punchline: Wendy's aside — 'A whole raisin' — reveals she is still hung up on the raisin incident, delivered with quiet horror
jokes.explanation: A small callback beat. 'A whole raisin.' delivered mid-toast as a horrified afterthought is a good bit of character comedy. Economy is excellent — three words, lots of character.

jokes.text: To your first job. / Ooh, my first job was actually a gap kids campaign when I was six. / To your first job!
jokes.setup: Stevie toasts to David's first job
jokes.punchline: David corrects her — he had a Gap Kids campaign at age six — implying he considers child modeling equivalent work experience; Stevie ignores the distinction entirely
jokes.explanation: The Gap Kids campaign as David's first 'job' is perfect character comedy — elite enough to be irrelevant to the current situation, casual enough to be treated as equivalent. Stevie's second 'To your first job!' cutting off his correction is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Tell me a story. / How about a song? / No! / A story it is.
jokes.setup: A sick Alexis asks Moira for comfort
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately tries to make it about a song (performance) — Alexis refuses — and Moira concedes with deadpan grace
jokes.explanation: Quick beat showing Moira's default impulse is always performance. The negotiation is brief and character-specific. Not a big laugh but warm.

jokes.text: There once was a radiant young actress, who dreamed of having two sons. The second of which would be named Alex.
jokes.setup: Moira tells Alexis a bedtime story
jokes.punchline: The story is clearly about Moira herself — and reveals that Alexis was supposed to be a boy named Alex — making Alexis's entire existence an accidental rebranding
jokes.explanation: Moira's bedtime story is transparently autobiographical and reveals Alexis was unplanned as a daughter — the name 'Alex' as the intended boy's name is a dark but funny detail. Classic Moira selfishness wrapped in maternal tenderness.

jokes.text: I don't like this story.
jokes.setup: Moira's story reveals Alexis was an unplanned daughter replacing a desired son
jokes.punchline: Alexis's flat 'I don't like this story' is the perfect reaction — recognizing exactly what's being revealed while being too tired to protest fully
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy for a perfect reaction. Five words that convey everything — Alexis understands, she's hurt and also amused, and Moira is oblivious. The understatement is exactly right.

jokes.text: Now it seems the vivacious daytime Emmy nominee was surprised by how taken she was with this unanticipated daughter. Mostly because she didn't cry as much as the unmannerly son, but also because she was adventurous, carefree, so beautiful. Just like her mother.
jokes.setup: Moira continues her thinly-veiled autobiography as a bedtime story
jokes.punchline: The compliment to Alexis is real but wrapped in multiple layers of Moira: (1) Moira characterizes herself as a daytime Emmy nominee, (2) David is 'unmannerly,' (3) the beauty compliment ends with 'Just like her mother' — so it's ultimately about Moira
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered moment. The compliment is genuinely meant but filtered through Moira's ego at every turn: the Emmy credentials, the dig at David (unmannerly son), and the circling back to 'just like her mother.' It's touching AND hilarious simultaneously. This is the episode's emotional and comedic peak. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Mostly because she didn't cry as much as the unmannerly son, but also because she was adventurous, carefree, so beautiful. Just like her mother.
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining why she came to love Alexis in the story.
jokes.punchline: 'Mostly because she didn't cry as much as the unmannerly son' — David is described as 'unmannerly' and Alexis's chief virtue is crying less — and then Moira concludes with 'just like her mother,' making the story's moral about Moira's vanity.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke. David is 'the unmannerly son' (a callback to the existing sibling dynamic), Alexis's highest praise is 'didn't cry as much,' and the whole thing ends with Moira complimenting herself ('just like her mother'). The bedtime story is ostensibly about Alexis but is really about Moira. Very funny and very true to character.

jokes.text: Alexis, you're on my arm. / Alexis, seriously. I can't feel it! I can't-Alexis!
jokes.setup: After a tender bedtime story, the scene ends with Alexis having fallen asleep on Moira's arm
jokes.punchline: Moira's increasing panic about her arm going numb breaks the tender scene's spell hilariously — she can't bear even this small physical discomfort for her child
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the tender bedtime story and Moira's immediate panic about physical inconvenience is a strong comedic beat. The warm moment is punctured by Moira's self-centeredness, which is the show's signature emotional-comedic move.

jokes.text: Go to sleep, dear.
jokes.setup: Alexis wakes up startled by Moira's arm panic
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Go to sleep, dear' — delivered after the commotion she herself caused — is a perfectly arch dismissal, as if Alexis is the one disrupting things
jokes.explanation: Four words, perfectly delivered. Moira caused the disruption and now dismisses its victim. The economy is flawless; the character integration is very strong.

jokes.text: Ray, I have always seen you as a sensitive, cultured man of the world. / Thank you. / And I'm sure your family wanted more for you when they immigrated from... / Winnipeg. / Winnipeg?
jokes.setup: Moira flatters Ray with 'cultured man of the world' while building to something — presumably an exotic origin story (Italy? France?)
jokes.punchline: His family immigrated from Winnipeg — a Canadian city, not an exotic locale — completely deflating Moira's romantic construction of him
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing joke is excellent. The setup beautifully misdirects toward exotic origins; 'Winnipeg' is the perfect punchline — it's Canadian enough to be mundane yet specific enough to be funny. Moira's echoed 'Winnipeg?' captures her horror at the ordinariness of it. The structure is textbook misdirection with near-perfect execution. Justifies 8+.

tmdb_overview: Johnny talks himself into an unlikely business partnership with Bob. David gets an interview at a woman's clothing store, while Moira reluctantly cares for an ailing Alexis.
insight: Bob's bagel business dream fuels 45 jokes through pure character-driven cringe.
